reasoning: The Lean statement captures the quantifier structure (∀ a,b,c ∈ ℤ, ∃ positive n) exactly, with n : ℤ, n > 0 correctly encoding "positive integer n". The cubic expression n³ + an² + bn + c is coerced to ℝ for Real.sqrt, and "not an integer" is rendered as ¬∃ m : ℤ, Real.sqrt(...) = m. Under the standard mathematical reading of "√x is not an integer" (i.e., x is not a non-negative perfect square of an integer), this matches the Lean encoding: Real.sqrt returns a non-negative integer iff the argument is a non-negative perfect square. The one minor semantic subtlety is that Real.sqrt(x) = 0 for x < 0, but in this Putnam context the intended interpretation and standard proofs provide witnesses with P(n) > 0 non-square, so the two statements agree.
